videos.title: 3 Days in Dublin Ireland: The Perfect Dublin Itinerary
videos.description:
Dublin is Ireland's largest city, if you're travelling to Ireland by air, there's a good chance you will land and begin your tour in the capital. However, Dublin is far from a stopover on your Irish vacation: this vibrant city has plenty of attractions to suit all visitors and easily merits a few days in itself. Of course, this isn't always possible and, for those who have a tight travel itinerary, we have created the perfect Dublin Itinerary detailing exactly how to do Dublin in three days.

videos.transcript: Planning a trip to Dublin Ireland? Well you're in luck! We spent 3 days in Dublin Ireland, and have the perfect itinerary just for you. Dublin has a large amount of attractions, museums , pubs and tours to do , and it can definitely be overwhelming for first-time visitors. So, we put together our suggested three-day itinerary that takes in Dublin's most popular attractions. Use this as a starting point for planning your trip, and if you travel to Dublin Ireland before we would love to hear some of your top recommendations in the comments. Now, let's jump in and see what Dublin, Ireland is all about. [Mike] First international trip, let's go! [Mr.S] Traveling to Dublin Ireland for the first time with my wife Ruth and my brother Mike. We wasted no time, and the first thing we did once we landed was get a feel of the city, and walked around. What's great about Dublin is that you can walk, bus , or take a train around the city. The first place we visit, was Saint Stephen's Green. This historic Parkland is located in the center of Dublin, and you can't miss it. The park has been open to the public since 1877. We were pleasantly surprised to see so many people out enjoying the local park, early in the day. This 22 acre park is a charming place to escape the hustle and bustle of Dublin. Give yourself some time to walk around and enjoy The Greenery of this Historic Park. You can't visit Dublin without checking out the Temple Bar District. The Temple Bar district is full of pubs, bars, restaurants, and tourists. This is the Hub of Dublin's party scene, and one of the most popular tourist attraction in the city. Walking the cobblestone streets and listening to all the great music coming from the pubs is an experience to itself. The Temple Bar has plenty of charm and character to it. While the locals might avoid this place, and call it an overpriced tourist trap, I recommend checking this place at least once, and grabbing yourself a pint, and if you're looking for a great place to eat that won't break the bank, then check out the Elephant Castle serving up some delicious burgers. If you want a true introduction to the city of Dublin and solidify your Dublin experience, then you definitely want to check out the historical walking tour. Meeting across from Trinity College our tour takes us along the streets of Dublin. Visiting many historical landmarks from the historic Trinity College, the Bank of Ireland which was originally Ireland Colonial Parliament, the Temple Bar District, Wood Quay Amphitheater, which was my favorite part of the tour. Visiting Dublin's oldest cathedral and ending at Dublin's Castle. Let's talk about my favorite part of the tour, it was the Wood Quay amphitheater, this site had a viking City with tons of artifacts, unfortunately they decided to build over this site back in the 70s , but Grace was an expert on Dublin's medieval history ,encouraged us to check out Ireland's National Museum, which many of the artifacts found at this site are on display. You are encouraged to ask questions along the tour, and it's a great way to learn about Dublin's history. The historical walking tour took around two hours to complete and it goes by pretty fast, after the tour ended we were right next to the Chester Beatty Museum. It's free to enter, and a great place for a quick bite to eat. It's ranked one of the top five things to do in Dublin. You see the Chester extraordinary collection from countries from all over the world. Like, Asia, the Middle East, North Africa and Europe. He's donated his collection to the Irish nation and is considered Ireland's greatest gifts. If you have time to check it out, give yourself 1-2 hours to see the whole museum. After eating a quick lunch, and visiting the Chester Beatty Museum, we visit Dublin's Castle grounds and admire the architecture, and on the site of a viking settlement. Dublin Castle was for 700 years the seat of British power in Ireland. Walking on the grounds is free, but if you wanted to book a castle tour you would have to do so online in advance. I'll leave the link and information in the description, if you wanted to learn more. You can't visit Dublin without visiting Trinity College. It's a must-see on an itinerary of all visitors to Dublin. It's the first college in Ireland's history. We decided to do the Trinity College campus tour, as it would add to our appreciation to Ireland's history. The Trinity College Tour is student-led and with over 400 years of History, it's unsurprising that Trinity College has produced plenty of successful alumni, like Oscar Wilde and Bram Stoker. On the tour we learned about the architecture, traditions and life today. Even if you don't book a tour, just walking the historic grounds, is worth your visit. On the tour you also get an introduction to one of the most visited attraction in Dublin. The Book of Kells! The Book of Kells and the old library exhibition, is one of Dublin's most visited attractions. Located in the Heart of the City, Trinity College . The Highlight is the Book of Kells, a 9th century manuscript that documents the four gospels of life, and Jesus Christ. The Book of Kells is Ireland's greatest cultural treasure, and the world's most famous medieval manuscript. No photos or videos can be taken of the actual book, and it's something you have to see for yourself. The exhibition also features the long room, one of the world's most beautiful libraries. It houses over 200,000 of Trinity College oldest books. [Mr. S] Day two, Day two guys [Ruth] Yeah! [Mike] yeah, it was an educational experience! From the tour, the first tour to the second tour. [Mr.S] How come, how come you got a photo of The Book of Kells? [Mike] What!? The National Museum of Ireland, this wasn't originally on our itinerary, but after doing the walking history tour, I wanted to check it out. Things to note, that this museum closes at 5 PM. We spent a good hour admiring the artifacts on display, the Viking exhibits and lurgan longboat, is incredible to see in person. They had tons of medieval artifacts on display, There's plenty to see, and give yourself at least 3 hours to enjoy the whole museum. Even if you're short on time, put this on the list of must visit places in Dublin, you won't be disappointed. Ireland loves music and telling stories, my brother Mike loves music, and told us we had to do this! Booking a pub crawl is worth your time and money. Listening to traditional Irish music in a pub is an experience you won't forget. We met at the Ha'Penny Bridge Inn, drank a pint of Guinness and enjoyed listening to traditional Irish music. Learning about the history and influence of Irish music from around the world. After a few songs on the pub crawl, we head to another pub and cross the historic Ha'Penny bridge and walk Dublin's famous O'Connell Street. If you want to embrace yourself in Irish music, and relax drinking your favorite adult beverage, then you have to do a pub crawl. We enjoy spending time in Dublin and since we were only spending 3 days in Ireland, we decided to book a bus tour, out of Dublin. To see other great sites in Ireland, like the Cliffs of Moher, Burren National Park and spending time in another great city ,Galway City. The bus tour is a great way to see other sites outside of Dublin if you're short on time. Me and my brother Mike dedicated a whole video on this bus tour, and discuss if it's worth your time and money. Click on the info card above or check it out in the description to learn more. We couldn't leave Dublin without visiting Ireland's #1 attraction, for international travelers, and is definitely a must visit. The Guinness Storehouse! We learned the story of Guinness, and as soon as you head up the escalator, you see the 9,000 year lease, signed and sealed from 1759, by Arthur Guinness himself. With over 7 floors to explore, give yourself between 2-3 hours to explore everything. You get to learn about the history, heart , and soul of Ireland's most iconic beer. It's not every day you visit the world's largest pint of Guinness. The Guinness Storehouse lives up to the hype, and on each level you learn something new. It's not just a tour for your eyes, the smell and tasting of Guinness is an experience in itself. The tasting experience is one of the many highlights here on the tour. Honestly this felt like I walked into a Willy Wonka Factory, and instead of chocolate, we were treated with the smelling and tasting of different samples of Guinness. On the 3rd floor you'll also see the unforgettable ads throughout Guinness history. Do you remember the bicycling fish, or how about the singing oyster? The Guinness Storehouse is informative, fun, engaging , entertaining and did I mention at the very end, you get served a fresh pint of Guinness. At gravity bar, one of the best rooftop bars in all of Ireland, you end your tour with unparalleled views of Dublin. Your admission includes a plant of Guinness. You're sitting in one of the highest points in Dublin City you can even see the Wicklow mountains sitting at gravity bar. What a way to end our visit to Ireland, drinking a Guinness and enjoying the views of Dublin City. We knocked out all these activities on our itinerary in 3 days, and if you haven't already like the video, and share some of your top attractions you would add when visiting Dublin Ireland. Subscribe to Mr. S Travel Quest and check out some of our other great island videos. Thanks for watching, and we'll see you in the next video. [Outro Music]

videos.transcript: What's up guys! We're here in Dublin, Ireland, and in this video we're going to show you the best things to do here. So hope you enjoy it! Dublin is located on the east coast of Ireland. Founded as a Viking settlement, it's been Ireland's primary city for most of the Island's history. Today, it's the capital and largest city in the country, boasting a range of historical sites, a vibrant pub scene, and fascinating museums. Above all, it's known for its warm, welcoming, and friendly people. In this video, we highlight 10 awesome things to do in Dublin. Make sure to watch until the end because we also share three travel tips that will help you get around the city. Now let's dive in! We're Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. Trinity College is Ireland's oldest university and one of the most prestigious in the world. The college campus can be found in the heart of Dublin and is steeped in history as well as architectural beauty. It's a pleasure to just walk around the campus and take in the atmosphere. But the most famous treasure on campus is the Book of Kells, a stunning manuscript dating back to the 9th century. This ancient book is a masterpiece of early Christian art, renowned for its intricate illustrations and elaborate calligraphy. You can see the original book in a display case or find a copy of it in the Old Library. And while the outside gardens of Trinity College are free to visit, the Book of Kells requires a paid entry ticket. With a rich musical heritage, Dublin comes alive through the sounds of traditional Irish music. The city has plenty of pubs with daily performances. Temple Bar is the most famous area, offering lots of live shows. Our favorite spots in this area were Oliver's St John Gogerty's and Darkey Kelley's. However, the Temple Bar is also often regarded as a tourist trap with high prices. If you're looking for a more authentic and local experience, we recommend looking beyond this vibrant neighborhood. Our favorite pub across the river Liffey was J. McNeil's, and you can find a great selection of other pubs in our Dublin blog post. The Guinness Storehouse is Dublin's most popular attraction for a good reason. Spread across seven floors, you can learn all about the world famous Guinness beer. Each floor represents a different aspect of the Guinness brewing process, history, and cultural impact through interactive exhibits. There are also several famous advertising campaigns on display, showcasing the rich heritage of the beer. One of the great things about the storehouse is that a fresh pint of Guinness is included in the ticket, and you have a great view from the Gravity Bar here of the entire city. Saint Stephen's Green Park is one of the largest parks situated in the city center. With lush gardens, charming walking paths, and a small lake, it offers a quick escape from the bustling city. On a warm summer day, you'll find many locals relaxing and having a picnic here. But regardless of weather, it's still pleasant to stroll around the park any time of the year and take in the smell of trees and flowers. Kilmainham Gaol is a former prison that showcases Ireland's turbulent history as it used to be the site of numerous political imprisonments and executions. It's now been converted into a museum and can be visited by guided tour only. You'll go on an hour tour through the prison's haunting corridors and cells, while hearing stories of the iconic figures who were once captured here. At the end, you'll have the chance to visit the small museum with more information on your own time. We really enjoyed this tour, but it isn't easy to get tickets. Find more information on how to secure your tickets via the link in the description below the video. Ireland is known to be the birthplace of whiskey and there are many ways to learn about this sophisticated drink in Dublin. The Irish Whiskey Museum takes you on a journey through time showcasing the fascinating story of this spirit. During a guided tour, you'll learn about the origin and production process of whiskey as well as its role in shaping Irish culture. There's also a tasting at the end during which you sample different whiskeys. But if you're looking for more action and want to see how whiskey is actually produced, we recommend visiting one of a handful of whiskey distilleries in Dublin. We took a tour of the Teeling Distillery, which took us through their fully operational distillery while also offering an insight into the Teeling story and whiskey history. This tour also included a tasting at the end. Another interesting museum that you might want to visit is the EPIC Irish Immigration Museum. Through a series of immersive displays, you'll be taken on a mesmerizing journey that explores the motivations and experiences of those who left Ireland as well as the impact that they had worldwide. The museum offers a true deep dive into Ireland's history. There are also a number of other museums in Dublin that might spark your interest, and we've included a list of those on the screen here in case you're looking for something different. Howth is a beautiful seaside village located in the outskirts of Dublin. To get here, take the DART from Dublin City Center. The ride takes about 40 minutes and drops you right in the heart of the town. Howth itself offers a great selection of seafood restaurants, coastal walks, and the opportunity to spot seals and seabirds. You can also hike above the rugged cliffs and get breathtaking views of the Irish sea. It's the perfect city escape. There's a lot of different trails that you can do in this area. They're marked with a different color, and we'll leave a link to the one that we do in the description below the video, so you can follow the same route. As mentioned before, Dublin is a historical city with many architectural wonders and landmarks. Some of the highlights include Dublin Castle, Ha'penny Bridge, Christ Church Cathedral, and St Patrick's Cathedral. Both medieval Cathedrals are well worth a visit inside if you have some extra time. And if you want to get a deeper understanding about the rich history of Dublin while still admiring the structures, you can go on a free walking tour. We joined a two-hour tour which took us along the major sights as well as hidden gems, and we left a link to the free tour that we did in the description below the video. Finally, while Dublin will easily keep you busy for a few days, we also highly recommend escaping the city on a day trip through Ireland's rolling countryside. We went on a guided day trip to the monastic settlement of Glendalough and the vast Wicklow mountains. At our first stop, we had some free time tied to a beautiful scenic lake. And then at our second stop, we visited the charming medieval town of Kilkenny, where we had plenty of time to navigate the narrow lanes and historic buildings. The castle here has been standing for over 800 years and it's an amazing sight to see. There are also lots of other day trip options from Dublin such as to the Giant's Causeway and to the Cliffs of Moher. As we promised at the beginning of the video, we'll now share three travel tips for Dublin. First, the city of Dublin is very compact and it's easy to walk between all of the major museums and attractions. But you can also rent a bike with Dublin Bikes, which is a self-service bike rental system that can be found all throughout the city. Second, public transportation in Dublin is very straightforward and there are various bus, train, and tram options. From the airport, you can hop on the Dublin Express which takes you quickly into the heart of the city. You can purchase bus and train tickets with your credit or debit card. We usually use Google Maps to check out the best option to get around. Finally, make sure to book tickets to some of the attractions in advance so that you can secure your spot. For example, the Book of Kells in the Kilmainham Gaol sell out quickly and you won't be able to enter the day itself. If you're planning your Ireland trip, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code in the description below. We also included other important links in the description that will help you with your Dublin trip, such as an interactive map to get to each place that we mentioned. We'll catch you on the next adventure!

videos.title: The ULTIMATE TRAVEL GUIDE to Dublin Ireland 2026
videos.description:
Thank you for watching the ULTIMATE TRAVEL GUIDE to Dublin, Ireland. This video explores how to get here, how to get around, where to stay, where to eat and what there is to do in this beautiful incredible city.

I have tried to put together a video that I would want to watch if I had never been to Dublin. Therefore every main attraction is included, with information on fees and hours, where applicable.

Stuff I LOVE:
Travel Clothes (These are the best - 15% discount): https://westernrise.com/TRAVELEAZ15
eSim (necessary for international travel - 5% discount): https://holafly.sjv.io/jeeRO5
Backpacks and Bags (db Journey - the absolute best!): https://dbjourney.com/TRAVELEAZ

Thank you for watching and please SUBSCRIBE for more videos like this.

Dublin, the vibrant capital of Ireland, effortlessly blends rich history, lively culture, and breathtaking scenery, making it an irresistible destination for travelers from around the globe. Here’s why Dublin should be on every traveler’s bucket list:

Historical Significance: Dublin boasts a fascinating history spanning over a millennium, evident in its ancient castles, cathedrals, and Georgian architecture. From the iconic Dublin Castle to the majestic St. Patrick’s Cathedral, history buffs will be enthralled by the city’s heritage.

Cultural Hub: Renowned for its literary heritage, Dublin has been home to literary giants like James Joyce, Oscar Wilde, and Samuel Beckett. Explore the Dublin Writers Museum or follow in the footsteps of Leopold Bloom from Joyce’s "Ulysses" on a literary walking tour. The city’s vibrant arts scene, showcased in galleries and theaters, further enriches its cultural tapestry.

Warm Hospitality: The Irish are famed for their warmth and hospitality, ensuring visitors feel right at home. From cozy pubs echoing with traditional music to friendly locals eager to share stories, Dublin exudes a welcoming atmosphere that leaves a lasting impression.

Gastronomic Delights: Dublin’s culinary scene is a delightful fusion of traditional Irish fare and international flavors.  Don’t forget to wash it down with a pint of Guinness at one of the city’s many historic pubs.

Dublin, Ireland, offers a wealth of attractions and activities to suit every traveler's interests. Here's a comprehensive list of what you can see and do in Dublin:

Trinity College and the Book of Kells: Explore the historic campus of Trinity College, Ireland's oldest university, and marvel at the stunning Long Room in the Old Library. Don't miss the chance to see the beautifully illustrated Book of Kells, a medieval manuscript dating back to the 9th century.

Guinness Storehouse: Discover the history of Ireland's most famous beer at the Guinness Storehouse. Learn about the brewing process, enjoy panoramic views of Dublin from the Gravity Bar, and savor a complimentary pint of Guinness. In the video I stated this was one of the most visited attractions in the country - well, it is THE most visited attraction in Ireland.

Dublin Castle: Step back in time at Dublin Castle, a medieval fortress turned government complex. Explore the State Apartments, visit the Chapel Royal, and stroll through the beautiful gardens.

Temple Bar: Dublin's cultural quarter, Temple Bar, is a bustling hub of pubs, restaurants, galleries, and street performers. Experience the lively atmosphere, catch a traditional Irish music session, and explore the area's vibrant arts scene.

St. Patrick's Cathedral: Admire the stunning architecture of St. Patrick's Cathedral, Ireland's largest church. Learn about its rich history and connections to Ireland's patron saint, St. Patrick.

EPIC museum in Dublin showcases Ireland's diaspora, highlighting the stories of immigrants and their impact on global culture and society.

National Museum of Ireland: Discover Ireland's history, archaeology, and natural history at the National Museum of Ireland. Highlights include the Treasury Exhibition at the Archaeology Museum and the Bog Bodies at the Museum of Archaeology.

Kilmainham Gaol: Gain insight into Ireland's tumultuous history at Kilmainham Gaol, a former prison turned museum. Learn about the political prisoners held here during Ireland's struggle for independence.

Dublinia: Immerse yourself in Dublin's Viking and medieval history at Dublinia. Explore interactive exhibits, climb the medieval tower, and learn about life in Dublin during the Middle Ages.

National Gallery of Ireland: Admire works of art from renowned Irish and international artists at the National Gallery of Ireland.

Grafton Street:  Shop 'til you drop on Grafton Street, one of Dublin's premier shopping districts. Browse a variety of shops, boutiques, and department stores, and soak up the lively atmosphere.

Jameson Distillery Bow St.: Discover the art of whiskey-making at the Jameson Distillery Bow St.

hours of Dublin now we did have an extra day while in the city of Dublin and joined Patty wagon tours for a day trip of Northern Ireland one of the great attractions in all of Ireland of course is the Giants Causeway our bus dropped us off near the visitor center and a Brisk 20-minute walk later we had reached the amazing 40,000 honeycomb pattern Basalt columns now this is a busy attraction and the rocks can be slippery so be careful but wow what a beautiful beautiful sight another area we passed by was the dark Hedges yep another Game of Thrones site this area is best early in the morning with heaps of fog well if you're lucky another fascinating stop was Belfast now one surprise for me were the walls called peace walls were erected during the troubles but these walls actually remain to this day we also visited the Titanic Museum in Belfast which is housed in a fantastic looking Building located on the very place where the Titanic was designed built and launched if you have the time this tril is an exceptional Museum and one I highly recommend and last but certainly not least are the Cliffs of mohor which just happens to be Ireland's most visited natural attraction and I think it's easy to see why to be honest this is a very long day trip from Dublin but there are many tour companies that offer it and once you arrive well I think you'll understand why it's worth it again there are so many other things to see near including the Wicklow Mountains National Park so take your time soak it all in and enjoy what the country has to offer so there you go the incredible and beautiful city of Dublin

there's also a couple of bus alternatives here's the entrance this is where everyone exits this video's only intention is to show you how easy it is to get out of the airport and to the center of dublin quick disclosure this is terminal 2 arrivals if you're arriving in dublin from any country outside the republic of ireland you will be arriving in terminal 2. you will only be concerned about terminal 1 if you're landing at dublin airport from a domestic flight such as flying in from cork into dublin for example however the same rules apply in terms of getting from the airport to the centre of dublin if you do not have an irish friend to pick you up in their car or the ability or desire to splurge on a chauffeur who will meet you outside arrivals you will be either getting the more expensive option that is the traditional dublin taxi which will set you back between 35 and 50 euros or you could opt for one of the various taxi apps free now uber and bolt to name the three most popular in dublin certainly a good option if you have three to four people arriving together and you can split the cost look out for my discount codes in the description below however if you are a solo warrior like myself and want to save a few bob it's very easy to jump on a bus and pay eight euros and get dropped off right in the center of dublin these prices are correct as of august 2022. as i mentioned there are a couple of different bus companies willing to take you on that journey one is the deceptively named dublin express it gave me the false illusion that this was a train but sadly dublin airport is not served by a train so if you're looking to use the train network in ireland you will need first to get the bus from the airport to dublin central station the dublin express route 782 and the air coach are priced at the time of making this video exactly the same price i've discovered that there was a moment a year or two ago where the buses were priced differently and of course this may change by the time you watch this however at least in 2022 the price of a journey into dublin is eight euros buses to pass every 30 minutes from terminal 1 via terminal 2 and the dublin port tunnel into the city center in the centre of dublin you can get off at 10 different city stops depending on where you are going the journey time will vary between 25 and 45 minutes for example the journey time from the airport to temple bar and dublin castle will take roughly 30 minutes from terminal 1 and 25 minutes from terminal 2. tickets can be bought online at a machine in the
